Were You Asked This?
Design a Metrics Aggregator

Design a metrics aggregation system that collects count-based metrics (like user signups, system errors, ad clicks) from services via a client library and displays them as histogram data on a dashboard. The system should support querying aggregated metrics within specific time frames for monitoring and analytics purposes.

Asked at:

DoorDash

OpenAI

Rippling

LinkedIn

+7 More

A StatsD/Datadog-style metrics aggregation platform that lets services emit count-based events (e.g., user_signups, system_error, ad_click) via a lightweight client library. The backend ingests a high-volume firehose, aggregates counts into time buckets, and powers dashboards that show histograms and time-series charts with filters by tags and time ranges.

Interviewers ask this to test your ability to design high-throughput, low-latency ingestion and aggregation pipelines, reason about windowed counters, hot-key contention, and storage layouts for time-series. It also probes trade-offs around consistency vs availability, real-time vs batch rollups, cardinality control, and how you expose fast queries without overwhelming the write path.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to instrument services with a client library to emit count metrics with optional tags (e.g., env=prod, region=us-east).

Users should be able to view near–real-time histograms and time-series graphs for selected metrics over a chosen time range and resolution (e.g., 1s, 10s, 1m).

Users should be able to query aggregated counts filtered by metric name and tags to support monitoring and analytics use cases.

Users should be able to retrieve aggregated metrics within specific time frames with predictable latency, even under high write load.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you handle very high write throughput and hot keys while keeping aggregation latency low?

What is your correctness model for counts under failures, retries, and duplicates?

How will you model storage and rollups to support fast queries at multiple time granularities?

How will you control metric and tag cardinality to prevent unbounded growth and cost blowups?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Scaling Writes

Metrics ingestion is write-heavy and bursty. Decoupling producers from consumers, batching increments, and horizontally sharding aggregation are essential to sustain millions of events per second while meeting near–real-time SLAs.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

Popular metrics create hot keys. Using sharded counters, striped keys, and per-bucket aggregation avoids single-point contention and keeps latency low under skewed traffic.

Read More
Scaling Reads

Dashboards execute many range queries across time windows. Pre-aggregated rollups, caches for hot windows, and read-friendly schemas are required to deliver consistent, low-latency queries.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka provides a durable, horizontally scalable buffer between client libraries and aggregators. It smooths ingestion spikes, enables backpressure, supports consumer groups for parallel aggregation, and can be replayed for recomputation or corrections.

Read More
Redis

Redis is ideal for low-latency, sharded in-memory counters per time bucket. It supports atomic increments, expiring keys, and Lua scripts for merging/flush operations, making it perfect for near–real-time aggregation before persisting.

Read More
Cassandra

Cassandra fits high-write, time-series workloads with wide-row designs partitioned by metric+tags and time buckets. It offers predictable write throughput, TTLs for retention, and efficient range scans for time-window queries.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Ad Click Aggregator

Both ingest a massive stream of simple events and produce time-windowed counts by dimensions (campaign, region, device). The same sharded counters, rollups, and hot-key mitigation apply.

Read
Practice
Rate Limiter

Rate limiting relies on accurate, low-latency, windowed counters across distributed nodes. Techniques like time-bucketed keys, atomic increments, and contention mitigation directly transfer.

Read
Practice
YouTube Top K

Finding trending items over time involves aggregations, rollups, and sometimes approximate techniques under heavy write loads. The storage and streaming patterns are closely related.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Writing every event directly to a transactional database without pre-aggregation or buffering

This creates severe write amplification, hot rows, and poor query performance. Under bursty load, it will blow SLAs and may cause outages due to lock contention and storage saturation.

Using a single global counter per metric leading to hot key contention

A single counter becomes a bottleneck and limits horizontal scale. Under skew, increments serialize, latency spikes, and the system cannot meet near–real-time requirements.

Ignoring duplicates, retries, and late events

Without a clear correctness model and dedupe strategy, counts will drift, dashboards will be misleading, and incident response will suffer. Interviewers expect at-least-once with bucket-level idempotence or periodic reconciliation.

Design a real-time analytics dashboard for Twitter that provides content creators and businesses with insights about their tweet performance. The system should display metrics like impressions, likes, retweets, replies, and engagement rates in real-time.  
* Display real-time metrics for tweets (impressions, likes, retweets, replies, clicks) * Show engagement rates and trends over time * Provide user-level analytics (follower growth, total impressions) * Support time-based filtering (last hour, day, week, month) * Export analytics data in various formats

Performance Requirements: * Handle 500M tweets per day with real-time metric updates * Support 10M concurrent dashboard users * Display metrics with <5 second latency for recent data * 99.9% availability during peak traffic

scale:
Global user base across multiple continents * Handle viral content spikes (tweets reaching 100M+ impressions) * Support enterprise customers with high-volume analytics needs

10TB/hr of data being sent from a lot of services. there are 3 main flows - 
1. teams should be able to see the data in real time for debugging 
2. The team can get exact data by issuing a regex query to the logs in real time.
3. The analytics team can look at data from 24 - 48 hrs, which hey receive in form of a tar file.

Mid October, 2024

Stripe

Mid-level

Essentially ad click aggregator but you are tabulating certain metrics data like "user signups" or "system_error" so they can be displayed on front end. The company can query on different metrics data to see how many events occurred in a given time frame.
